title: Chapter 17: Supply Lines
content: The key to Phase Two was the vending machine on Floor 7.

It sat in the east hallway, halfway between the stairwell and the fire escape, a glass-fronted monument to the convenience economy that had sustained office workers in happier times. According to Luna's reconnaissance, it was fully stocked—chips, candy, water, sandwiches, energy drinks. Enough calories to feed Derek's group for a week.
We couldn't let him have it.

"The machine's in an open hallway," Mia said, studying the floor plan I'd sketched. "No cover on approach. If Derek's watching the stairwell from Floor 8—which he is, I've seen him—he'll spot us the second we enter the corridor."
"What if we go through the fire escape?"

"Fire escape access is on the opposite end of the hall. You'd have to cross twenty feet of open floor to reach the machine, clear it, and haul the supplies back. Minimum exposure time: three minutes. Derek could reach Floor 7 in ninety seconds if he runs."
"So we need more than ninety seconds of distraction."

"Or we need to be in and out before he knows we're there." Mia traced the route with her finger. "What if Luna goes first? She moves like smoke—Derek's never spotted her. She clears the floor, signals clear, then you and I haul the goods."
"What about the zombies on Floor 7?"

"That's the beauty of it. I've been cleaning them out from above—sniper fire through the stairwell windows. Floor 7 is nearly clear. The few that remain are dormant, standing sleepers. Luna can handle them silently."
I looked at the plan. It was solid—aggressive, fast, high-risk but with a real chance of success. Military thinking, applied to apartment building logistics.
"When?" I asked.
"Tomorrow. 4 AM. Derek's a heavy sleeper after his whiskey, and the darkness covers our approach. We hit the machine, strip it clean, and vanish before dawn."
"4 AM it is."
The raid went sideways at 4:17 AM.

Luna went first—down the stairwell, through the Floor 7 door, into the darkened hallway. The stairwell windows let in just enough moonlight to see shapes, and Luna's enhanced vision made her navigation effortless. I followed two minutes later, Mia covering our rear with her rifle from the Floor 8 landing—she'd positioned herself to intercept any threat from Derek's direction.

The hallway was quiet. Eerie quiet. The vending machine's glass front reflected the moonlight like a promise, and I could see the stock inside—rows of chips, candy bars hanging from coils, water bottles lined up like soldiers.
Luna appeared at my elbow, silent as ever. "Floor is clear. Two sleepers in the far hallway. Not moving."
"Let's work."

We hit the machine in pairs. Mia had brought duffel bags—salvaged from a floor we'd cleared earlier—and we loaded them with everything that would fit. Chips, candy, water, energy bars, the remaining sandwiches that were only slightly past their expiration date. My hands moved fast, my brain cataloguing each item with the same intensity I'd once brought to inventory management in RPGs.
Two minutes in. Half the machine cleared.
Then Luna's head snapped up.
"Sound," she whispered. "Footsteps. Above us."
Above was Floor 8. Derek's floor.
"Movement?" I hissed.
"Two people. Coming down. Fast."
Mia was already at the stairwell door, rifle up. "How many rounds do I have?"
"Seven," I said. She'd told me earlier—she'd used four on zombies and kept the rest for emergencies.
"Enough. Get the bags and go. I'll cover your retreat."
"Mia—"
"Go, Alex. I'll be right behind you."

There was no time to argue. I grabbed two duffel bags—each weighing thirty pounds—and ran for the fire escape. Luna grabbed the remaining two and vanished ahead of me, her speed making my best sprint look like a stroll.
Behind me, the stairwell door burst open.
Derek Voss, pistol drawn, stepped onto Floor 7 with Rick right behind him, a hunting knife in his grip.
"STOP!"
I didn't stop. I was already at the fire escape door, shoving it open with my shoulder, the cold night air hitting my face like a slap.
A gunshot. The bullet struck the wall six inches from my head, spraying concrete dust into my eyes. I staggered, blind, my arms burning under the weight of the supplies—

Luna's hand caught my collar. She *lifted* me—both me and the bags, one-armed—and threw us both through the fire escape door. I hit the metal landing with a clang that echoed through the stairwell, and Luna was already up, already positioning herself between me and the door, the fire axe in her hands.
"You come through this door," she said, her voice carrying through the metal framework, "and I will end you."
Silence from the other side. Then Derek's voice, ragged with fury:
"This isn't over, Chen. Not even close."
A door slammed—the stairwell door closing, Derek retreating. Tactical withdrawal. He'd have to be stupid to engage Luna in close quarters, and whatever else Derek was, stupid wasn't on the list.
Mia emerged from the shadows, her rifle slung low. "He's gone back up. Rick too."
"Everyone okay?" I asked, spitting concrete dust.
"Present and accounted for," Mia said.
Luna nodded. Her eyes were still fixed on the door, her body thrumming with combat readiness, but she was uninjured.

"We got the supplies," I said, looking at the four duffel bags scattered on the fire escape landing. Chips, candy, water, sandwiches—enough to sustain us for another week, and more importantly, enough that Derek wouldn't have them.
"Cost of doing business," Mia said, eyeing the bullet hole in the wall. "He's down to three rounds now."
Three rounds. One less threat.
Small comfort, but I'd take it.
The next morning, I woke to Sarah shaking my shoulder.
"Alex. Wake up. Something's happening."
I was out of bed before my eyes fully opened—combat reflexes I didn't know I had, honed by a week of sleeping with one ear open.
"What is it?"
She pointed at the window. I looked.

The courtyard below was in motion. Not zombie motion—there were zombies, as always, shuffling in their aimless patterns—but something else. A group of people, maybe eight or ten, moving through the street in formation. They had weapons. They had supplies. They moved with the coordinated confidence of a group that had been together long enough to develop tactics.
At their head was a man I'd never seen before—big, bearded, wearing a tactical vest that probably wasn't for show.
And next to him, pointing toward the building, was Derek Voss.

**SYSTEM ALERT: EXTERNAL THREAT DETECTED**
**THE WAREHOUSE CLAN — Armed Raider Group**
**SIZE: 8-10 combatants**
**THREAT LEVEL: Extreme**
**NOTE: Derek Voss has made contact with external hostile forces. Siege conditions no longer apply. Prepare for direct assault.**
My blood ran cold.
Derek hadn't been waiting. He'd been *recruiting*.
And the reinforcements had arrived.
